rows.definition_en: Electrical pressure that causes current to flow; measured in volts (V)

rows.definition_en: Flow of electrons through a conductor; measured in amperes (A)
rows.definition_es: Flujo de electrones a través de un conductor; medido en amperios (A)

rows.definition_en: Opposition to current flow; measured in ohms (Ω)
rows.definition_es: Oposición al flujo de corriente; medido en ohmios (Ω)

rows.definition_en: Rate of energy consumption; measured in watts (W)
rows.definition_es: Tasa de consumo de energía; medida en vatios (W)

rows.definition_en: Electric current that reverses direction periodically
rows.definition_es: Corriente eléctrica que invierte su dirección periódicamente

rows.definition_en: Electric current that flows in one direction only
rows.definition_es: Corriente eléctrica que fluye en una sola dirección

rows.definition_en: Tool for measuring voltage, current, and resistance
rows.definition_es: Herramienta para medir voltaje, corriente y resistencia

rows.definition_en: Safety procedure to ensure machines are properly shut off
rows.definition_es: Procedimiento de seguridad para asegurar que las máquinas estén apagadas correctamente

rows.definition_en: Safety gear worn to minimize exposure to hazards
rows.definition_es: Equipo de seguridad usado para minimizar la exposición a peligros

rows.definition_en: Explosive release of energy from an electrical fault

rows.definition_en: Industrial digital computer for automation control
rows.definition_es: Computadora digital industrial para control de automatización

rows.definition_en: PLC programming language resembling electrical circuits

rows.definition_en: Component that permits rotation while supporting load
rows.definition_es: Componente que permite rotación mientras soporta carga

rows.definition_en: Device that moves fluids by mechanical action

rows.definition_en: Machine that converts electrical energy to mechanical energy
rows.definition_es: Máquina que convierte energía eléctrica en energía mecánica

Register new equipment from nameplate

View your work order status and history
Questions about equipment manuals and procedures
Get expert help with equipment issues

rows.title: How to test a fuse with a multimeter
rows.content: Set your multimeter to continuity mode (beep symbol). Place one probe on each end of the fuse. If the meter beeps, the fuse is good. If no beep, the fuse is blown. For glass fuses, you can also visually inspect — look for a broken wire inside. Always replace with the same amperage rating.

rows.title: Common fuse types in industrial applications
rows.content: Class CC: Motor circuits, very fast acting. Class J: General purpose, current limiting. Class T: High interrupting capacity. Class RK5: General purpose, time delay. Ferrule fuses: European standard, DIN rail mount. Always check voltage and current rating before replacement.

rows.title: Circuit breaker won't reset - troubleshooting steps
rows.content: 1. Turn breaker fully OFF before attempting to reset. 2. Check for short circuits downstream. 3. Disconnect all loads and try reset. 4. If resets with no load, reconnect loads one by one. 5. Check for ground faults with insulation tester. 6. Verify breaker rating matches load. 7. Replace breaker if mechanically damaged.

rows.title: Contactor chattering - causes and solutions
rows.content: Causes: Low voltage to coil, dirty contacts, worn spring assembly, loose connections. Solutions: Check coil voltage under load, clean contacts with appropriate cleaner, tighten all connections, replace worn contactors. AC coils need 85% rated voltage minimum. DC coils more sensitive to voltage drops.

rows.title: When to replace contactor contacts
rows.content: Replace when: Contact surfaces pitted >1mm, silver layer worn off, contacts welded together, arc damage visible, irregular contact surfaces. Measure contact resistance - should be <100 microohms. For safety contacts, replace at 50% wear. Always replace both sets (NO and NC) together.

rows.title: Motor overload relay selection and adjustment
rows.content: Select overload current rating at motor FLA (Full Load Amps). Class 10: Normal starting. Class 20: High inertia loads. Class 30: Very high starting torque. Adjust to motor nameplate FLA, not drive rating. For VFD applications, use electronic overload in drive or bypass mechanical overload.

rows.title: Transformer insulation testing procedure
rows.content: 1. De-energize and lock out transformer. 2. Disconnect all loads. 3. Test primary to secondary: Use 2500V megger, minimum 100 megohms. 4. Test primary to ground: minimum 100 megohms. 5. Test secondary to ground: minimum 100 megohms. 6. Test each phase to phase. Values below 1 megohm indicate insulation problems.

rows.title: Proper equipment grounding practices
rows.content: Use bare copper ground wire minimum #12 AWG. Bond all metal enclosures to ground. Equipment ground must be separate from neutral. Ground rod resistance <25 ohms per NEC. Use appropriate ground lugs - copper to copper only. Paint removal required at all bonding points. Test ground integrity annually.

rows.title: Safe voltage testing procedures
rows.content: 1. Use properly rated meter and leads. 2. Test meter on known source first. 3. Approach circuit with leads in hand, not in meter. 4. Test before, during, and after work. 5. Never assume circuit is de-energized. 6. Use lock-out/tag-out procedures. 7. Wear appropriate PPE. 8. Test meter after use to verify operation.

rows.title: Identifying power quality problems
rows.content: Symptoms: Equipment resets, motor heating, light flicker, nuisance trips. Use power quality analyzer. Look for: voltage sags/swells, harmonics, phase imbalance, transients. Common causes: Motor starting, switching loads, poor connections, utility issues. Solutions: Isolation transformers, harmonic filters, voltage regulators.

rows.title: Three-phase motor runs on two phases
rows.content: Symptoms: Motor hums, draws high current, may run slow. Causes: Blown fuse, open contactor contact, broken wire, failed motor winding. Test: Check voltage at motor terminals, measure winding resistance (should be equal ±5%). Solutions: Replace blown protection, repair wiring, rewind or replace motor if winding failed.

rows.title: Motor bearing replacement procedure
rows.content: 1. Photograph connections before disconnection. 2. Remove coupling/belts. 3. Mark bearing cap orientation. 4. Use proper bearing puller - never hammer. 5. Heat new bearing to 100°C max. 6. Install quickly while warm. 7. Use correct grease type and quantity. 8. Reassemble in reverse order. 9. Check alignment and balance.

rows.title: Motor insulation resistance testing
rows.content: Test with 500V megger for motors up to 1000V. Minimum resistance: 1 megohm + 1 megohm per 1000V rated voltage. Test phase-to-phase and phase-to-ground. Temperature affects readings - correct to 40°C standard. Values below minimum indicate moisture or insulation breakdown. Polarization index test: 10-minute/1-minute reading ratio should be >1.5.

rows.title: Motor vibration analysis basics
rows.content: 1X RPM: Unbalance. 2X RPM: Misalignment, bent shaft. Bearing frequencies: Inner race, outer race, ball defects have specific frequencies. High frequency random: Lubrication issues. Use vibration analyzer or smartphone app for basic screening. Velocity readings: <0.3 in/s excellent, >0.7 in/s poor.

rows.title: DC motor brush maintenance
rows.content: Replace brushes when worn to 1/3 original length or if commutator shows excessive wear. New brushes require 24-48 hour break-in at reduced load. Check brush spring pressure - typically 2-3 PSI. Polish commutator with fine sandpaper if needed. Never use emery cloth. Check for proper brush grade for application.

rows.title: Servo motor encoder troubleshooting
rows.content: Symptoms: Position errors, oscillation, faults. Check encoder power supply (typically 5V or 24V). Verify cable integrity - encoders sensitive to noise. Check for loose coupling. Test encoder output signals with oscilloscope. Common failures: Cable damage, encoder board failure, contamination on encoder disk.

rows.title: VFD motor auto-tune procedure
rows.content: 1. Enter correct motor nameplate data: voltage, current, frequency, RPM. 2. Set motor type (induction/synchronous). 3. Ensure motor is disconnected from load. 4. Run stationary auto-tune first. 5. For rotating auto-tune, ensure shaft can turn freely. 6. Save parameters after successful tune. 7. Verify motor runs smoothly at various speeds.

rows.title: Critical VFD parameter setup
rows.content: Motor nameplate data: Voltage, current, frequency, power factor. Acceleration/deceleration times: Start with 10 seconds, adjust based on load inertia. V/Hz curve: Linear for constant torque, quadratic for fans/pumps. Current limit: Set to 110% of motor FLA. Carrier frequency: 2-16kHz, higher reduces noise but increases losses.

rows.title: VFD overcurrent fault troubleshooting
rows.content: Check motor cable for shorts to ground or phase-to-phase. Verify motor insulation with megger test. Check acceleration time - may be too fast for load inertia. Verify motor parameters match nameplate. Check for mechanical binding in load. Inspect drive for contamination or damaged power modules.

rows.title: VFD overvoltage fault solutions
rows.content: Increase deceleration time to reduce regenerative energy. Add external brake resistor for high-inertia loads. Check input voltage - may be too high. Verify brake resistor wiring and resistance value. For elevator/crane applications, consider regenerative drives. Check DC bus capacitors for failure.

rows.title: VFD preventive maintenance schedule
rows.content: Monthly: Check cooling fans, clean air filters. Quarterly: Check torque on power connections, inspect for corrosion. Annually: Check DC bus capacitance, verify parameter backup, test emergency stop circuits. Every 3 years: Replace cooling fans. Every 5-7 years: Replace electrolytic capacitors in older drives.

rows.title: Digital input not working - troubleshooting steps
rows.content: 1. Check input status LED on module. 2. Verify field voltage at input terminals. 3. Check input device (switch, sensor) operation. 4. Test input wiring continuity. 5. Verify common terminal wiring. 6. Check input filter settings if available. 7. Test with known good input device. 8. Replace I/O module if confirmed faulty.

rows.title: Analog input reading errors
rows.content: Check signal wiring - use shielded cable for 4-20mA, twisted pair for voltage. Verify input scaling in PLC program. Check loop power for 2-wire transmitters. Test signal with calibrated meter at PLC terminals. Check for ground loops - shield grounded at one point only. Verify input module specifications match signal type.

rows.title: EtherNet/IP communication troubleshooting
rows.content: Check physical connections - use Cat5e minimum. Verify IP addresses are unique and on same subnet. Check for duplicate IP addresses with ping. Verify network switch ports and cables. Check PLC communication status LEDs. Use network scanner to verify device visibility. Check firewall settings if applicable.

rows.title: Modbus RTU troubleshooting
rows.content: Verify baud rate, parity, stop bits match on all devices. Check RS485 wiring polarity (A/B or +/-). Ensure proper termination resistors (120 ohm) at network ends. Verify device addresses are unique. Check for proper bias resistors on network. Use Modbus scanner tool to test communication.

rows.title: PLC program backup and documentation
rows.content: Always backup program before making changes. Document all program modifications with comments. Use descriptive tag names, avoid cryptic abbreviations. Create separate backup after each major change. Store backups off-site or in cloud storage. Include I/O list and wiring diagrams with backup. Test program changes in offline mode when possible.

rows.title: PLC battery replacement procedure
rows.content: Check battery voltage with PLC powered - should be near rated voltage. Power down PLC completely before battery change. Replace battery within 30 minutes to prevent memory loss. Use only manufacturer-specified battery type. Reset real-time clock after replacement. Upload program backup immediately after power-up to verify memory integrity.

rows.title: Safety light curtain installation and testing
rows.content: Install with minimum 6-inch safety distance from hazard. Use safety-rated control relays for outputs. Test daily with test piece sized per manufacturer specs. Check for proper alignment - all LEDs should be lit. Verify muting function only active when needed. Ensure bypass capability is key-locked and documented.

rows.title: Emergency stop circuit design requirements
rows.content: Use Category 3 or 4 safety circuit per ISO 13849. E-stop buttons must be red mushroom head, yellow background. Use dual-channel monitoring relays. Wire normally-closed contacts in series. Provide manual reset after E-stop activation. Test monthly for proper operation. Document all E-stop locations and functions.

rows.title: Safety relay selection and wiring
rows.content: Select appropriate safety category (B, 1, 2, 3, 4) based on risk assessment. Use dual-channel inputs for higher categories. Provide separate safety outputs for different functions. Use guided-contact relays for safety applications. Test safety relay function during commissioning and periodically. Follow manufacturer's wiring diagrams exactly.

rows.title: LOTO procedure for electrical equipment
rows.content: 1. Notify affected personnel. 2. Shut down equipment properly. 3. Identify all energy sources. 4. Isolate energy sources (breakers, valves). 5. Apply locks and tags. 6. Verify zero energy state. 7. Test equipment cannot start. 8. Perform work safely. 9. Remove LOTO devices only by person who applied them.

rows.title: Pneumatic air preparation unit maintenance
rows.content: Check filter bowl for water and debris daily. Replace filter element when pressure drop exceeds 5 PSI across filter. Adjust regulator to required pressure - typically 80-90 PSI for general use. Check lubricator oil level weekly. Use proper pneumatic oil - ISO VG 32. Drain water from filter bowl regularly.

rows.title: Pneumatic cylinder troubleshooting
rows.content: Slow operation: Check air pressure, valve flow capacity, cylinder seals. Erratic motion: Check for sticky valves, contaminated air, worn seals. No motion: Check air supply, valve operation, cylinder binding. Leaking: Check seals, rod surface finish, proper lubrication. Test cylinder manually when possible.

rows.title: Hydraulic pump maintenance procedures
rows.content: Check fluid level daily - maintain between min/max marks. Monitor operating temperature - should not exceed 180°F continuously. Check for external leaks at fittings and seals. Monitor pump pressure and flow. Change fluid and filters per manufacturer schedule. Check coupling alignment quarterly.

rows.title: Hydraulic filter maintenance and selection
rows.content: Return filter: 10-25 micron for general systems. Pressure filter: 3-10 micron for servo systems. Change filters when bypass indicator shows or pressure drop exceeds 25 PSI. Use proper filter media for fluid type. Check for water contamination with test strips. Maintain fluid cleanliness per ISO 4406 standards.

rows.title: MIG welder troubleshooting common problems
rows.content: Poor arc: Check wire feed speed, voltage setting, contact tip condition. Spatter: Reduce voltage or wire speed, check gas flow rate (15-25 CFH for CO2/Argon). Porosity: Check gas coverage, clean base metal, check for drafts. Wire burning back: Replace contact tip, check liner condition, verify proper wire tension.

rows.title: Welding equipment preventive maintenance
rows.content: Daily: Check gas levels, inspect cables for damage. Weekly: Clean spatter from gun, check wire feed rollers. Monthly: Check contact tips, inspect liner for wear. Annually: Check transformer taps, test ground fault protection, verify cooling system operation. Keep spare consumables in stock.

rows.title: Conveyor belt tracking adjustment
rows.content: Belt tracking to one side: Adjust idler on side belt is tracking toward - turn bolt clockwise to push belt away. For head pulley tracking: If belt tracks right, move right side of head pulley forward. Check for material buildup on pulleys. Verify belt is not damaged or stretched unevenly.

rows.title: Conveyor bearing replacement and lubrication
rows.content: Replace bearings when temperature exceeds 160°F or vibration increases significantly. Use proper bearing puller - never hammer bearings. Grease sealed bearings lightly - over-greasing causes premature failure. Check belt tension after bearing replacement. Verify proper alignment of pulleys and idlers.

rows.title: Centrifugal pump troubleshooting guide
rows.content: No flow: Check suction valve open, prime pump, verify rotation direction. Low flow: Check for cavitation (NPSH), worn impeller, clogged strainer. High vibration: Check coupling alignment, impeller balance, bearing condition. Seal leakage: Check seal faces, spring pressure, flush system operation.

rows.title: Positive displacement pump maintenance
rows.content: Check relief valve setting - should be 10-25% above normal operating pressure. Monitor pump case pressure for internal leakage. Replace wear plates when clearances exceed specifications. Check packing or seal condition regularly. Verify proper suction line size to prevent cavitation.

rows.title: Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 — Fault F001: External Fault
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 Fault F001: External Fault
External fault input active
Possible causes: External fault contact closed, Wiring fault to terminals, External device fault
Recommended actions: Check external fault contacts, Verify field wiring, Check external device status

rows.title: Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 — Fault F002: Auxiliary Input
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 Fault F002: Auxiliary Input
A fault condition was detected on the auxiliary input
Possible causes: External fault signal active, Wiring issue on aux input, Incorrect parameter setting
Recommended actions: Check external fault signal, Verify wiring to aux input terminal, Review aux input parameter

rows.title: Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 — Fault F003: Motor Overload
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 Fault F003: Motor Overload
Motor thermal overload protection activated
Possible causes: Motor overloaded, Incorrect motor parameters, Blocked motor shaft, High ambient temperature
Recommended actions: Check motor load, Verify motor nameplate data, Check for mechanical binding, Improve ventilation

rows.title: Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 — Fault F004: Heatsink Overtemp
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 Fault F004: Heatsink Overtemp
Drive internal temperature too high
Possible causes: Blocked air flow, High ambient temperature, Fan failure, Excessive switching frequency
Recommended actions: Clean heatsink, Check fan operation, Reduce switching frequency, Improve ventilation

rows.title: Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 — Fault F005: I²t Overload
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 Fault F005: I²t Overload
Current squared time overload protection
Possible causes: Sustained overcurrent, Motor stalled, Load too high, Incorrect current limit
Recommended actions: Reduce motor load, Check for stalled condition, Verify current limit setting, Check motor coupling

rows.title: Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 — Fault F006: DC Bus Overvoltage
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 Fault F006: DC Bus Overvoltage
DC bus voltage exceeded safe limits
Possible causes: Regenerative load, Input voltage too high, Deceleration too fast, Brake resistor failure
Recommended actions: Increase deceleration time, Check input voltage, Verify brake resistor, Add external brake resistor

rows.title: Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 — Fault F007: DC Bus Undervoltage
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 Fault F007: DC Bus Undervoltage
DC bus voltage below minimum operating level
Possible causes: Input voltage too low, Input phase loss, Power supply issue, Incoming power disturbance
Recommended actions: Check input voltage, Verify all input phases, Check power supply, Check incoming power quality

rows.title: Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 — Fault F008: DC Bus Ripple
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 Fault F008: DC Bus Ripple
Excessive ripple on DC bus voltage
Possible causes: Input phase loss, Unbalanced input voltage, Weak power source, DC bus capacitor failure
Recommended actions: Check all input phases, Verify input voltage balance, Check power source capacity, Test DC bus capacitors

rows.title: Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 — Fault F009: Output Phase Loss
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 Fault F009: Output Phase Loss
Loss of one or more output phases to motor
Possible causes: Motor cable fault, Motor winding fault, Output terminal loose, Contactor failure
Recommended actions: Check motor cables, Test motor windings, Tighten output terminals, Check output contactor

rows.title: Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 — Fault F010: Motor Phase Current Imbalance
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 525 Fault F010: Motor Phase Current Imbalance
Unbalanced current between motor phases
Possible causes: Motor winding fault, Cable connection issue, Motor bearing failure, Mechanical load imbalance
Recommended actions: Test motor windings, Check cable connections, Test motor bearings, Check mechanical coupling

rows.title: Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 755 — Fault F011: Output Overcurrent
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 755 Fault F011: Output Overcurrent
Drive output current exceeded maximum limit
Possible causes: Motor short circuit, Ground fault, Incorrect motor parameters, Load surge
Recommended actions: Check for motor short, Test for ground faults, Verify motor parameters, Check load conditions

rows.title: Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 755 — Fault F012: Ground Fault
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley PowerFlex 755 Fault F012: Ground Fault
Ground fault detected on motor or cable
Possible causes: Motor insulation failure, Cable insulation fault, Moisture in motor, Contamination
Recommended actions: Test motor insulation, Check cable insulation, Dry motor if wet, Clean contamination

rows.title: Allen-Bradley CompactLogix — Fault 16#0001: General Hardware Fault
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley CompactLogix Fault 16#0001: General Hardware Fault
Unspecified hardware fault detected
Possible causes: Module failure, Power supply issue, Backplane problem, Environmental stress
Recommended actions: Replace module, Check power supply, Verify backplane connections, Check environment

rows.title: Allen-Bradley CompactLogix — Fault 16#0002: No Configuration
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley CompactLogix Fault 16#0002: No Configuration
Module has no valid configuration
Possible causes: Configuration not downloaded, Module mismatch, Corrupted configuration, Wrong module type
Recommended actions: Download configuration, Verify module type, Recreate configuration, Replace module if needed

rows.title: Allen-Bradley CompactLogix — Fault 16#0004: Module Fault
rows.content:
Allen-Bradley CompactLogix Fault 16#0004: Module Fault
I/O module reported a fault condition
Possible causes: Module hardware failure, Wiring fault, Field device fault, Power supply issue
Recommended actions: Check module diagnostics, Verify wiring, Test field devices, Check 24V supply

rows.title: Siemens SINAMICS G120 — Fault F0001: Overcurrent
rows.content:
Siemens SINAMICS G120 Fault F0001: Overcurrent
Output current limit exceeded
Possible causes: Motor short circuit, Ground fault, Load too high, Acceleration too fast
Recommended actions: Check motor for shorts, Test insulation, Reduce load, Increase ramp time

rows.title: Siemens SINAMICS G120 — Fault F0002: Overvoltage
rows.content:
Siemens SINAMICS G120 Fault F0002: Overvoltage
DC link voltage too high
Possible causes: Regenerative energy, Supply voltage too high, Deceleration too fast, Brake resistor fault
Recommended actions: Increase decel time, Check supply voltage, Check brake resistor, Add brake resistor

rows.title: Siemens SINAMICS G120 — Fault F0003: Undervoltage
rows.content:
Siemens SINAMICS G120 Fault F0003: Undervoltage
DC link voltage too low
Possible causes: Supply voltage low, Phase failure, Power outage, Line reactor too large
Recommended actions: Check supply voltage, Check all phases, Check power quality, Verify line reactor

rows.title: Siemens SINAMICS G120 — Fault F0004: Overtemperature
rows.content:
Siemens SINAMICS G120 Fault F0004: Overtemperature
Drive temperature too high
Possible causes: Blocked cooling, Ambient too hot, Fan failure, Overload condition
Recommended actions: Clean cooling channels, Reduce ambient temp, Replace fan, Reduce load

rows.title: Siemens SINAMICS G120 — Fault F0005: Motor overload
rows.content:
Siemens SINAMICS G120 Fault F0005: Motor overload
Motor thermal protection activated
Possible causes: Motor overloaded, Blocked motor, Wrong motor data, High ambient temperature
Recommended actions: Reduce motor load, Check for blockage, Verify motor parameters, Improve cooling

rows.title: Siemens SINAMICS S7-1200 — Fault F0010: Communication fault
rows.content:
Siemens SINAMICS S7-1200 Fault F0010: Communication fault
Communication error with control unit
Possible causes: Cable fault, Bus termination missing, EMI interference, Wrong baud rate
Recommended actions: Check bus cable, Verify termination, Check EMI shielding, Set correct baud rate

rows.title: ABB ACS550 — Fault 2210: OVERCURRENT
rows.content:
ABB ACS550 Fault 2210: OVERCURRENT
Output current too high
Possible causes: Short circuit in motor cable, Ground fault, Wrong motor data, Too high load
Recommended actions: Check motor and cable, Check motor data, Reduce load or increase drive size

rows.title: ABB ACS550 — Fault 2220: EARTH FAULT
rows.content:
ABB ACS550 Fault 2220: EARTH FAULT
Ground fault in motor or motor cable
Possible causes: Damaged motor cable, Motor insulation failure, Moisture in motor
Recommended actions: Check cable insulation, Dry motor, Replace damaged cable

rows.title: ABB ACS550 — Fault 2230: OVERVOLTAGE
rows.content:
ABB ACS550 Fault 2230: OVERVOLTAGE
DC intermediate circuit voltage too high
Possible causes: Too high supply voltage, Regenerating load, Deceleration too fast
Recommended actions: Check supply voltage, Increase deceleration time, Use braking resistor

rows.title: ABB ACS550 — Fault 2240: UNDERVOLTAGE
rows.content:
ABB ACS550 Fault 2240: UNDERVOLTAGE
DC intermediate circuit voltage too low
Possible causes: Too low supply voltage, Missing supply phase, Blown input fuse
Recommended actions: Check supply voltage and phases, Check input fuses, Check supply quality

rows.title: ABB ACS880 — Fault 3210: IGBT TEMP
rows.content:
ABB ACS880 Fault 3210: IGBT TEMP
IGBT temperature too high
Possible causes: High ambient temperature, Blocked air flow, Fan failure, Overload
Recommended actions: Reduce ambient temperature, Clean air filters, Check fan, Reduce load

rows.title: Checkpoint Frequency Tuning
rows.summary: Save checkpoints every 5 minutes for long tasks, every major phase completion for structured work
rows.content:
**Practice**: Tune checkpoint frequency based on task duration and complexity.

**Guidelines**:
- **Long-running tasks** (>30 min): Every 5 minutes (time-based)
- **Structured tasks**: Every phase completion (event-based)
- **Short tasks** (<10 min): Only on completion

**Implementation**: Set checkpointInterval in pai-config settings.json. Time-based triggers via setInterval, event-based triggers via hooks.

**Cost Tradeoff**: More checkpoints = more disk I/O but less lost work. Fewer checkpoints = faster but riskier.

**Why It Matters**: Checkpoint too frequently → slow performance, unnecessary disk writes. Checkpoint too rarely → risk losing significant work on crash.

**Monitoring**: Track checkpoint file sizes, disk usage, restoration frequency.

**Tools**: pai-config checkpoint system, settings.json

rows.title: Cross-Repository Configuration Management
rows.summary: Environment variables + structured config files pattern shared across Agent-Factory (database), Backlog.md (YAML), pai-config (JSON)
rows.content:
**Problem**: All 3 repos need runtime configuration without code changes.

**Solutions**: Each repo uses appropriate config mechanism with environment variable fallback.

**Agent-Factory**: Database-backed (agent_factory_settings table) with env fallback. settings.get("KEY", category) → DB → ENV → default.

**Backlog.md**: YAML config (backlog/config.yaml) with structured milestones, paths. Loaded on server start.

**pai-config-windows**: JSON config (settings.json) with hooks, checkpoint settings. TypeScript strongly typed.

**Shared Principle**: Environment variables as fallback + structured config files for complex settings. Single source of truth per setting.

**Anti-Pattern**: Duplicate configuration across repos. Always use one source (usually pai-config) that propagates via env vars.

**Files**: agent_factory_settings table (Supabase), backlog/config.yaml, settings.json

rows.title: One Task In Progress Policy
rows.summary: Maintain exactly ONE task in In Progress state to ensure focus and completion
rows.content:
**Practice**: Always complete current task before starting next. Exactly ONE task "In Progress" at any time.

**Why It Matters**: Task thrashing (starting multiple tasks without finishing) leads to nothing getting done. Serial execution ensures completion.

**Workflow**:
1. Check current task: backlog.task_list({status: "In Progress"})
2. If exists: Complete it before starting new task
3. Mark new task In Progress
4. Work until done
5. Mark Done
6. Repeat

**Exception**: Blocked tasks. If current task is blocked, mark it "To Do" with blocker note, then start next.

**Enforcement**: Workflow reminder in Backlog.md, not hard-enforced (allows flexibility for edge cases).

**Benefits**: Clear focus, guaranteed progress, easy status reporting ("What's current?" has one answer).

**Tools**: Backlog.md task_list, task_edit MCP tools

rows.title: Multi-Provider Database Failover
rows.summary: PostgreSQL failover across 3 providers (Supabase, Railway, Neon) achieves 99.9% uptime vs 99.5% single-provider
rows.content:
**Problem**: Single PostgreSQL provider (Supabase) had outages, breaking agent memory and knowledge base access.

**Solution**: Support 3 PostgreSQL providers with automatic failover, health checks, and connection pooling.

**Architecture**: DatabaseManager maintains connection pools for all providers, performs health checks (60s cache), and automatically fails over when primary is unavailable.

**Providers**:
- Supabase (Free tier, pgvector, PostgREST API)
- Railway ($5/month, 1GB, better uptime)
- Neon (Serverless, auto-scale)

**Failover Strategy**: Try primary → health check fails → try next in sequence. Environment variable DATABASE_FAILOVER_ORDER defines sequence.

**Performance**: Single provider 99.5% uptime (4 hours downtime/month), multi-provider 99.9% uptime (40 minutes downtime/month). Failover adds +200ms latency (acceptable for async agents).

**Files**: agent_factory/core/database_manager.py (452 lines)

rows.title: Test Atom
rows.summary: Test Summary
rows.content: Test content for KB-001

rows.title: SME Agent Template Pattern
rows.summary: Abstract base class for domain expert agents reduces implementation from 200 lines to 50 lines (75% code reduction)
rows.content:
**Problem**: Each domain expert agent (Motor Control, PLC Programming, Networking) had 200+ lines of duplicate boilerplate.

**Solution**: SMEAgentTemplate abstract base class enforces standard structure. Developers implement 4 methods: analyze_query(), search_kb(), generate_answer(), score_confidence().

**Architecture**: Template method pattern with fixed workflow: Query → Analysis → KB Search → Answer Generation → Confidence Scoring → Escalation Decision.

**Standard Workflow**:
1. analyze_query() extracts domain, entities, keywords, complexity
2. search_kb() performs semantic search with reranking
3. generate_answer() builds context and generates via LLM
4. score_confidence() combines document relevance, answer length, keyword overlap
5. Escalate if confidence < threshold

**Benefits**: 75% less code (200→50 lines), consistent error handling, automatic escalation, standardized metadata, rapid development (30 min vs 2 hours).

**Files**: agent_factory/templates/sme_agent_template.py (375 lines), docs/patterns/SME_AGENT_PATTERN.md (365 lines), examples/sme_agent_example.py (261 lines)

rows.title: Observability Stack for AI Agents
rows.summary: Distributed tracing + metrics + structured logging provides production visibility for 50+ autonomous agents
rows.content:
**Problem**: 50+ autonomous agents running 24/7 with no visibility into failures, costs, or performance.

**Solution**: Implement distributed tracing (spans), metrics collection (histograms, counters, gauges), and structured logging.

**Architecture**: Tracer.start_span() creates root span → Nested spans for LLM calls, RAG search, database queries → Each span has attributes (model, tokens, cost, duration) → Export to backend (Jaeger, Grafana).

**Key Features**:
1. Distributed Tracing: Track requests across multiple agents, visualize dependencies
2. Metrics Collection: Operation duration histograms, error rate counters, cost gauges
3. Structured Logging: JSON logs with metadata (query, confidence, sources, latency_ms)
4. Cost Attribution: Track LLM costs per agent for budgeting

**Usage**: Wrap operations in spans, set attributes for context, export to monitoring backend.

**Files**: agent_factory/observability/tracer.py (300 lines)

rows.title: LLM Cost Tracking Best Practices
rows.summary: Track LLM costs per request with automatic aggregation, export to dashboards, and set budget alerts
rows.content:
**Practice**: Always track LLM costs at request level and aggregate for monitoring.

**Implementation**:
1. Use LLMRouter's automatic cost tracking (every response includes usage.total_cost_usd)
2. Aggregate with get_global_tracker().aggregate_stats()
3. Export metrics to monitoring dashboard (Grafana)
4. Set budget alerts (e.g., > $50/day)
5. Break down costs per agent for attribution

**Why It Matters**: Autonomous agents can rack up costs quickly. Daily monitoring prevents surprise bills and enables cost optimization.

**Real Example**: Agent-Factory tracked $750/month → identified expensive model usage → implemented routing → reduced to $198/month.

**Tools**: agent_factory/llm/tracker.py, Grafana dashboards, budget alerts

rows.content:
ATTENTION: An incorrectly applied or installed drive can result in 
component damage or a reduction in product life. Wiring or application 
errors, such as undersizing the motor, incorrect or inadequate AC supply, or 
excessive ambient temperatures may result in malfunction of the system. ATTENTION: The bus regulator function is extremely useful for preventing 
nuisance overvoltage faults resulting from aggressive decelerations, 
overhauling loads, and eccentric loads. However, it can also cause either of 
the following two conditions to occur. 1. Fast positive changes in input voltage or imbalanced input voltages can 
cause uncommanded positive speed changes. 2. Actual deceleration times can be longer than commanded deceleration 
times. However, a “Stall Fault” is generated if the drive remains in this state for one 
minute. If this condition is unacceptable, the bus regulator must be 
disabled (see parameter A550 [Bus Reg Enable]). In addition, installing a 
properly sized dynamic brake resistor will provide proper stopping 
requirements based on braking resistor sizing. ATTENTION: Risk of injury or equipment damage exists. Drive does not 
contain user-serviceable components. Do not disassemble drive chassis. 14

Unshielded

ar
rows.content:
This helps minimize the 
possible shock hazard from “cross coupled” power leads. Chapter 1 Installation/Wiring
Rockwell Automation Publication 520-UM001O-EN-E - September 2025
Use copper wire only. Wire gauge requirements and recommendations are 
based on 75 °C (167 °F). Do not reduce wire gauge when using higher 
temperature wire.
Unshielded
THHN, THWN, or similar wire is acceptable for drive installation in dry 
environments provided adequate free air space and/or conduit fill rates limits 
are provided. Any wire that is chosen must have a minimum insulation 
thickness of 15 mils and should not have large variations in insulation 
concentricity.
Shielded/Armored Cable
Shielded cable contains all general benefits of multi-conductor cable with the 
added benefit of a copper braided shield that can contain much of the noise 
that is generated in the distribution system. Applications with large numbers 
of drives in a similar location, imposed EMC regulations or a high degree of 
communications / networking are also good candidates for shielded cable.
Shielded cable may also help reduce shaft voltage and induced bearing 
currents for some applications. In addition, the increased impedance of 
shielded cable may help extend the distance that the motor can be located from 
the drive without the addition of motor protective devices such as terminator 
networks. See Reflected Wave in the Wiring and Grounding for Pulse Width 
Modulated (PWM) AC Drives Installation Instructions, 
publication DRIVES-IN001.
Consideration should be given to all general specifications dictated by the 
environment of the installation, including temperature, flexibility, moisture 
characteristics, and chemical resistance. In addition, a braided shield should 
be included and be specified by the cable manufacturer as having coverage of 
at least 75%. An additional foil shield can greatly improve noise containment.
A good example of recommended cable is Belden 295xx (xx determines gauge). 
This cable has four XLPE insulated conductors with a 100% coverage foil and 
an 85% coverage copper braided shield (with drain wire) surrounded by a 
PVC jacket.
Other types of shielded cable are available, but the selection of these types may 
limit the allowable cable length. Particularly, some of the newer cables twist 
four conductors of THHN wire and wrap them tightly with a foil shield. This 
construction can greatly increase the cable charging current required and 
reduce the overall drive performance. Unless specified in the individual 
WARNING: The distance between the drive and motor must not exceed the 
maximum cable length that is stated in the Motor Cable Length Restrictions 
Tables in the Wiring and Grounding for Pulse Width Modulated (PWM) AC Drives 
Installation Instructions, publication DRIVES-IN001.
ATTENTION: Do not use THHN or similarly coated wire in wet areas.

Chapter 2
Startup
This chapter describes how to startup the PowerFlex 520-series drive. To 
simplify drive setup, the most commonly programmed parameters are 
organized in a single Basic Program Group.
 
Prepare for Drive Startup
Drive Startup Task List
1.
Disconnect and lock out power to the machine.
2. Verify that AC line power at the disconnect device is within the rated 
value of the drive.
3.
If replacing a drive, verify the current drive’s catalog number. Verify all 
options installed on the drive.
4. Verify that any digital control power is 24 volts.
5.
Inspect grounding, wiring, connections, and environmental 
compatibility. 
6. Verify that the Sink (SNK)/Source (SRC) jumper is set to match your 
control wiring scheme. See the PowerFlex 523 Control I/O Terminal 
Block on page 38 and PowerFlex 525 Control I/O Terminal Block on 
page 42 for location.
7.
Wire I/O as required for the application.
8. Wire the power input and output terminals.
9. Confirm that all inputs are connected to the correct terminals and are 
secure.
10. Collect and record motor nameplate and encoder or feedback device 
information. Verify motor connections.
• Is the motor uncoupled?
• What direction will the motor need to turn for the application?
IMPORTANT
Read the General Precautions on page 13 section before proceeding.
ATTENTION: Power must be applied to the drive to perform the following 
startup procedures. Some of the voltages present are at incoming line 
potential. To avoid electric shock hazard or damage to equipment, only 
qualified service personnel should perform the following procedure. 
Thoroughly read and understand the procedure before beginning. If an 
event does not occur while performing this procedure, Do Not Proceed. 
Remove All Power including user supplied control voltages. User supplied 
voltages may exist even when main AC power is not applied to the drive. 
Correct the malfunction before continuing.
IMPORTANT
The default control scheme is Source (SRC). The Stop terminal is 
jumpered to allow starting from the keypad or comms. If the control 
scheme is changed to Sink (SNK), the jumper must be removed from I/O 
Terminals 01 and 11 and installed between I/O Terminals 01 and 04.

Chapter 2 Startup
11. Verify the input voltage to the drive. Verify if the drive is on a grounded 
system. Ensure the MOV jumpers are in the correct position. See AC 
Supply Source Considerations on page 19 for more information.
12. Apply power and reset the drive and communicat
rows.content:
If the control 
scheme is changed to Sink (SNK), the jumper must be removed from I/O 
Terminals 01 and 11 and installed between I/O Terminals 01 and 04. 60
Rockwell Automation Publication 520-UM001O-EN-E - September 2025
Chapter 2 Startup
11. Verify the input voltage to the drive. Verify if the drive is on a grounded 
system. Ensure the MOV jumpers are in the correct position. See AC 
Supply Source Considerations on page 19 for more information.
12. Apply power and reset the drive and communication adapters to factory 
default settings. To reset the drive, see parameter P053 [Reset to 
Defaults]. To reset the communication adapters, see the user manual of 
the adapter for more information.
13. Configure the basic program parameters that are related to the motor. 
See Smart Startup with Basic Program Group Parameters on page 65.
14. Complete the autotune procedure for the drive. See parameter P040 
[Autotune] for more information.
15. If you are replacing a drive and have a backup of the parameter settings 
obtained using the USB utility application, use the USB utility 
application to apply the backup to the new drive. See Using the USB Port 
on page 67 for more information.
Otherwise, set the necessary parameters for your application using the 
LCD keypad interface, Connected Components Workbench™ software, 
or RSLogix™, or Studio 5000 Logix Designer application if using an 
Add-on Profile through EtherNet/IP.
• Configure the communication parameters needed for the application 
(node number, IP address, Datalinks in and out, communication rate, 
speed reference, start source, and so on). Record these settings for 
your reference.
• Configure the other drive parameters that are needed for the drive 
analog and digital I/O to work correctly. Verify the operation. Record 
these settings for your reference.
16. Verify that the drive and motor perform as specified.
• Verify that the Stop input is present or the drive does not start.
• Verify that the drive is receiving the speed reference from the correct 
place and that the reference is scaled correctly.
• Verify that the drive is receiving start and stop commands correctly.
• Verify that input currents are balanced.
• Verify that motor currents are balanced.
17. Save a backup of the drive settings using the USB utility application. See 
Using the USB Port on page 67 for more information.
Start, Stop, Direction, and Speed Control 
Factory default parameter values allow the drive to be controlled from the 
keypad. No programming is required to start, stop, change direction and 
control speed directly from the keypad.
If a fault appears on power-up, see Fault Descriptions on page 161 for an 
explanation of the fault code.
IMPORTANT
If I/O Terminal 01 is used as a stop input, the jumper between I/O 
Terminals 01 and 11 must be removed.
IMPORTANT
To disable reverse operation, see A544 [Reverse Disable].

1.1.5 
Residual risks of power drive systems 
The control and drive components of a drive system are approved for industrial and 
commercial use in industrial line supplies.
rows.content:
You will find further information at this address 
(http://www.siemens.com/industrialsecurity). 
• Make sure that you include all installed products into the holistic industrial security 
concept. Safety instructions 
 
1.1 Fundamental safety instructions 
SINAMICS V20 Inverter 
Operating Instructions, 09/2014, A5E34559884 
1.1.5 
Residual risks of power drive systems 
The control and drive components of a drive system are approved for industrial and 
commercial use in industrial line supplies. Their use in public line supplies requires a 
different configuration and/or additional measures. 
These components may only be operated in closed housings or in higher-level control 
cabinets with protective covers that are closed, and when all of the protective devices are 
used. 
These components may only be handled by qualified and trained technical personnel who 
are knowledgeable and observe all of the safety instructions on the components and in the 
associated technical user documentation. 
When assessing the machine's risk in accordance with the respective local regulations (e.g., 
EC Machinery Directive), the machine manufacturer must take into account the following 
residual risks emanating from the control and drive components of a drive system: 
1. Unintentional movements of driven machine components during commissioning, 
operation, maintenance, and repairs caused by, for example, 
– Hardware and/or software errors in the sensors, control system, actuators, and cables 
and connections 
– Response times of the control system and of the drive 
– Operation and/or environmental conditions outside the specification 
– Condensation/conductive contamination 
– Parameterization, programming, cabling, and installation errors 
– Use of wireless devices/mobile phones in the immediate vicinity of the control system 
– External influences/damage 
2. In the event of a fault, exceptionally high temperatures, including an open fire, as well as 
emissions of light, noise, particles, gases, etc. can occur inside and outside the inverter, 
e.g.: 
– Component failure 
– Software errors 
– Operation and/or environmental conditions outside the specification 
– External influences/damage 
Inverters of the Open Type/IP20 degree of protection must be installed in a metal control 
cabinet (or protected by another equivalent measure) such that contact with fire inside 
and outside the inverter is not possible. 
3. Hazardous shock voltages caused by, for example, 
– Component failure 
– Influence during electrostatic charging 
– Induction of voltages in moving motors 
– Operation and/or environmental conditions outside the specification 
– Condensation/conductive contamination 
– External influences/damage

Note 
The components must be protected against conductive contamination (e.g. by installing them

Assuming that conductive contamination at the installation site can definitely be excluded, a

Inverter disposal 
The packaging of the inverter is re-usable. Retain the packaging for future use. 
Easy-to-release screw and snap connectors allow you to break the unit down into its 
component parts. You can recycle these component parts, dispose of them in accordance 
with local requirements or return them to the manufacturer.

4.3 
EMC-compliant installation 
EMC-compliant installation of the inverter 
The shield connection kit is supplied as an option for each frame size (For more information 
about this option, see Appendix "Shield connection kits (Page 333)".). It allows easy and 
efficient connection of the necessary shield to achieve EMC-compliant installation of the 
inverter. If no shield connection kit is used, you can alternatively mount the device and 
additional components on a metal mounting plate with excellent electrical conductivity and a 
large contact area. This mounting plate must be connected to the cabinet panel and the PE 
or EMC bus bar. 
The following diagram shows an example of EMC-compliant installation of the inverter frame 
size B/C.

rows.title: The built-in Basic Operator Panel (BOP)

4.4 EMC-compliant cabinet design 
SINAMICS V20 Inverter 
Operating Instructions, 09/2014, A5E34559884 
4.4 
EMC-compliant cabinet design 
The most cost-effective method of implementing interference suppression measures within 
the control cabinet is to ensure that interference sources and potentially susceptible 
equipment are installed separately from each other. 
The c
rows.content:
Shielding method 
The following illustration shows an example with and without the shielding plate. Electrical installation 
 
4.4 EMC-compliant cabinet design 
SINAMICS V20 Inverter 
Operating Instructions, 09/2014, A5E34559884 
4.4 
EMC-compliant cabinet design 
The most cost-effective method of implementing interference suppression measures within 
the control cabinet is to ensure that interference sources and potentially susceptible 
equipment are installed separately from each other. 
The control cabinet has to be divided into EMC zones and the devices within the control 
cabinet have to be assigned to these zones following the rules below. 
● The different zones must be electromagnetically decoupled by using separate metallic 
housings or grounded separation plates. 
● If necessary, filters and/or coupling modules should be used at the interfaces of the 
zones. 
● Cables connecting different zones must be separated and must not be routed within the 
same cable harness or cable channel. 
● All communication (e.g. RS485) and signal cables leaving the cabinet must be shielded. Electrical installation

Appendix F
Encoder Usage and Position 
StepLogic Application
Encoder Usage
The PowerFlex 40P drive includes a pulse-train/encoder input. When 
E216 [Motor Fdbk Type] is set to a value other than zero, the drive is set 
to use an encoder. The drive will use the encoder in several ways 
depending on the settings of other parameters. The drive will use the 
encoder as shown below (listed in order of priority):
1. If enabled by P038 [Speed Reference], the encoder will be used 
directly as a commanded speed (normally used with a pulse train) or 
as a position reference (normally used with a quadrature encoder).
2. If not enabled by P038 [Speed Reference], when the PID Feedback 
or PID Reference are set to use an encoder than the PID function will 
use the encoder or pulse train input.
3. If not enabled by P038 [Speed Reference] or the PID parameters, 
then if E216 [Motor Fdbk Type] is set the encoder is used for direct 
feedback and trim of the speed command. The normal slip 
compensation is not used in this case. Instead the drive will use the 
encoder to determine actual output frequency and adjust the output 
frequency to match the command. Parameters E220 [Ki Speed Loop] 
and E221 [Kp Speed Loop] are used in this control loop. The 
primary benefit of this mode is increased speed accuracy when 
compared to open-loop slip compensation. It does not provide speed 
bandwidth improvement.

5.1.6 
LED states 
The SINAMICS V20 has only one LED for status indications. The LED can display orange, 
green, or red. 
If more than one inverter state exists, the LED displays in the following order of priority: 
● Parameter cloning 
● Commissioning mode 
● All faults 
● Ready (no fault) 
For example, if there is an active fault when the inverter is in the commissioning mode, the 
LED flashes green at 0.5 Hz. 
 
Inverter state 
LED color 
Power up 
Orange 
 
Ready (no fault) 
Green 
 
Commissioning mode 
Slow flashing green at 0.5 Hz 
 
All faults 
Fast flashing red at 2 Hz 
 
Parameter cloning 
Flashing orange at 1 Hz 
 
5.2 
Checking before power-on 
Perform the following checks before you power on the inverter system: 
● Check that all cables have been connected correctly and that all relevant product and 
plant/location safety precautions have been observed. 
● Ensure that the motor and the inverter are configured for the correct supply voltage. 
● Tighten all screws to the specified tightening torque.

rows.title: LLM Router Cost Optimization
rows.summary: Multi-provider LLM routing with capability-based model selection reduces costs 73% ($750/mo → $198/mo) for 50+ autonomous agents
rows.content:
**Problem**: Autonomous agents used expensive models (GPT-4o) for simple tasks like classification, causing unnecessarily high costs.

**Solution**: Route requests to the cheapest capable model based on task complexity using ModelCapability enum (SIMPLE, MODERATE, COMPLEX, CODING, RESEARCH).

**Architecture**: LLMRouter selects models from registry based on capability requirements. Supports 12 models across 4 providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Ollama) with automatic failover chain and exponential backoff.

**Key Features**:
- Multi-provider support via LiteLLM
- Fallback chain (max 3 models)
- Automatic cost tracking
- LRU cache with TTL (30-40% hit rate)
- Streaming support

**Impact**: 73% cost reduction in production with 50+ agents, from $750/month to $198/month.

**Files**: agent_factory/llm/router.py (493 lines), agent_factory/llm/config.py (347 lines)

5.5.1.3 
Setting connection macros 
 
 
NOTICE 
Connection macro settings 
When commissioning the inverter, the connection macro setting is a one-off setting. Make 
sure that you proceed as follows before you change the connection macro setting to a 
value different from your last setting: 
1. Do a factory reset (P0010 = 30, P0970 = 1) 
2. Repeat the quick commissioning and change the connection macro 
Failure to observe may cause the inverter to accept the parameter settings from both the 
currently and the previously selected macros, which may lead to undefined and 
unexplainable inverter operation. 
However, communication parameters P2010, P2011, P2021 and P2023 for connection 
macros Cn010 and Cn011 are not reset automatically after a factory reset. If necessary, 
reset them manually. 
After changing P2023 setting for Cn010 or Cn011, power-cycle the inverter. During the 
power-cycle, wait until LED has gone off or the display has gone blank (may take a few 
seconds) before re-applying power. 
Functionality 
This menu selects which macro is required for standard wiring arrangements. The default 
one is "Cn000" for connection macro 0. 
All connection macros only change the CDS0 (command data set 0) parameters. The CDS1 
parameters are used for the BOP control.

P033 [Motor OL Current]
Related Parameters: t069, t072, t076, t081, A484, A485, A493
Sets the motor nameplate overload current. Used to determine motor overload conditions and can be set from 0.1 A to 200% of drive rated current.
Values
Default:
Drive Rated Amps
Min/Max:
0.0/(Drive Rated Amps x 2)
Display:
0.1 A
IMPORTANT
The drive will fault on an F007 “Motor Overload” if the value of this parameter is exceeded based on class 10 motor 
overload protection according to NEC article 430 and motor over-temperature protection according to NEC article 
430.126 (A) (2). UL 508C File 29572.

No. 158
Rockwell Automation Publication 520-UM001O-EN-E - September 2025
Chapter 3 Programming and Parameters
Notes: Rockwell Automation Publication 520-UM001O-EN-E - September 2025
Chapter 4
Troubleshooting
This chapter provides information to guide you in troubleshooting the 
PowerFlex 520-series drive. Included is a listing and description of drive faults 
with possible solutions, when applicable.
Drive Status
The condition or state of your drive is constantly monitored. Any changes are 
indicated through the integral LCD display.
See Display and Control Keys on page 61 for information on drive status 
indicators and controls.
Faults
A fault is a condition that stops the drive. There are two fault types.
ATTENTION: Risk of injury or equipment damage exists. Drive does not 
contain user-serviceable components. Do not disassemble drive chassis.
Fault Types
Type
Fault Description
Auto-Reset/Run
When this type of fault occurs, and A541 [Auto Rstrt Tries] is set to a value greater 
than “0,” a user-configurable timer, A542 [Auto Rstrt Delay], begins. When the timer 
reaches zero, the drive attempts to automatically reset the fault. If the condition that 
caused the fault is no longer present, the fault is reset and the drive is restarted.
Non-Resettable
This type of fault may require drive or motor repair, or is caused by wiring or 
programming errors. The cause of the fault must be corrected before the fault can 
be cleared.

Connection macro Cn005 - Analog input and fixed frequency 
The analog input works as an additional setpoint. 
● If digital input 2 and digital input 3 are active together, the selected frequencies are 
summed, that is, FF1 + FF2 
 
Function diagram 
When the fixed speed is selected, the additional setpoint channel from the analog is 
disabled. If there is no fixed speed setpoint, the setpoint channel connects to the analog 
input.

control again.

Setting application macros 
 
 
NOTICE 
Application macro settings 
When commissioning the inverter, the application macro setting is a one-off setting. Make 
sure that you proceed as follows before you change the application macro setting to a value 
different from your last setting: 
1. Do a factory reset (P0010 = 30, P0970 = 1) 
2. Repeat the quick commissioning and change the application macro 
Failure to observe may cause the inverter to accept the parameter settings from both the 
currently and the previously selected macros, which may lead to undefined and 
unexplainable operation. 
Functionality 
This menu defines certain common applications. Each application macro provides a set of 
parameter settings for a specific application. After you select an application macro, the 
corresponding settings are applied to the inverter to simplify the commissioning process. 
The default application macro is "AP000" for application macro 0. If none of the application 
macros fits your application, select the one that is the closest to your application and make 
further parameter changes as desired.

rows.content:
A slave can never transmit without first 
being initiated by the master so that direct information transfer between individual slaves is 
not possible. Communicating with the PLC 
 
6.1 USS communication 
 
SINAMICS V20 Inverter 
Operating Instructions, 09/2014, A5E34559884 
The messages are always sent in the following format (half-duplex communication): 
 
● Response delay time: 20 ms 
● Start delay time: depends on baud rate (minimum operation time for 2-character string: 
0.12 to 2.3 ms) 
● Message transfer sequence: 
– master polls slave 1, then slave 1 responds 
– master polls slave 2, then slave 2 responds 
● Fixed framing characters that can not be altered: 
– 8 data bits 
– 1 parity bit 
– 1 or 2 stop bits

5.6.2 
Commissioning basic functions 
5.6.2.1 
Selecting the stop mode 
Functionality 
Both the inverter and the user have to respond to a wide range of situations and stop the 
inverter if necessary. Thus operating requirements as well as inverter protective functions 
(e.g. electrical or thermal overload), or rather man-machine protective functions, have to be 
taken into account. Due to the different OFF functions (OFF1, OFF2, OFF3) the inverter can 
flexibly respond to the mentioned requirements. Note that after an OFF2 / OFF3 command, 
the inverter is in the state "ON inhibit". To switch the motor on again, you need a signal low 
→ high of the ON command. 
OFF1 
The OFF1 command is closely coupled to the ON command. When the ON command is 
withdrawn, OFF1 is directly activated. The inverter is braked by OFF1 with the ramp-down 
time P1121. If the output frequency falls below the parameter value P2167 and if the time in 
P2168 has expired, then the inverter pulses are cancelled.

selected. This allows a process to be stopped and then restarted at the

Appendix F Encoder Usage and Position StepLogic Application
In all position modes, the following parameters will control the 
characteristics at each step:
E230…E244 [Step Units x]
These are the number value to the left of the decimal (whol
rows.content:
After start, the drive 
will move through the steps in the same manner as if option 2 was 
selected. This allows a process to be stopped and then restarted at the 
position where it stopped. 174 Rockwell Automation Publication 22D-UM001F-EN-E - September 2024
Appendix F Encoder Usage and Position StepLogic Application
In all position modes, the following parameters will control the 
characteristics at each step:
E230…E244 [Step Units x]
These are the number value to the left of the decimal (whole number) of 
the 8 positions desired for an application, beginning with Step 0 (E230) 
and continuing with each step until Step 7 (E244). For example, enter 2 
into this parameter if you would like a commanded position of 2.77. E231…E245 [Step Units F x]
These are the number value to the right of the decimal (the portion less 
than 1) of the 8 positions desired for an application, beginning with Step 
0 (E231) and continuing with each step until Step 7 (E245). For 
example, enter 0.77 into this parameter if you would like a commanded 
position of 2.77. A070…A077 [Preset Freq x]
In Positioning mode, each preset frequency parameter defines the 
maximum frequency the drive will run at during the corresponding step. For example, if [Preset Freq 2] is set to 40 Hz, the drive will accelerate to 
40 Hz maximum when moving to Position 2. Important: The default value for A070 [Preset Freq 0] is 0.00 Hz. This 
value needs to be changed or the drive will not be able to 
move during Step 0. A150…A157 [Stp Logic Time x]
In Positioning mode, each Step Logic Time parameter defines the time 
the drive will remain in each corresponding step if that step is 
time-based.

IMPORTANT
This setting is effective only if I/O that controls the drive is transmitted through the EtherNet/IP interface. ATTENTION: Risk of injury or equipment damage exists. Parameter C143 [EN Comm Flt Actn] lets you determine the 
action of the EtherNet/IP interface and connected drive if communications are disrupted. By default, this parameter 
faults the drive. You can set this parameter so that the drive continues to run. Precautions should be taken to ensure 
that the setting of this parameter does not create a risk of injury or equipment damage. When commissioning the 
drive, verify that your system responds correctly to various situations (for example, a disconnected drive).

Operating Instructions, 09/2014, A5E34559884 
5.6.2.2 
Running the inverter in JOG mode 
Functionality 
The JOG function can be controlled by either the (built-in) BOP or the digital inputs. When 
controlled by the BOP, pressing the RUN button will cause the motor to start and rotate at 
the pre-set JOG frequency (P1058). The motor stops when the RUN button is released. 
When using the digital inputs as the JOG command source, the JOG frequency is set by 
P1058 for JOG right and P1059 for JOG left. 
The JOG function allows: 
● to check the functionality of the motor and inverter after commissioning has been 
completed (first traversing motion, checking the direction of rotation, etc.) 
● to bring a motor or a motor load into a specific position 
● to traverse a motor, e.g. after a program has been interrupted 
 
Setting parameters 
 
Parameter 
Function 
Setting 
P1055[0...2] 
BI: Enable JOG right 
This parameter defines source of JOG right when P0719 = 0 
(Auto selection of command / setpoint source). 
Factory default: 19.8 
P1056[0...2] 
BI: Enable JOG left 
This parameter defines source of JOG left when P0719 = 0 
(Auto selection of command / setpoint source). 
Factory default: 0 
P1057 
JOG enable 
= 1: Jogging is enabled (default) 
P1058[0...2] 
JOG frequency [Hz] 
This parameter determines the frequency at which the inverter 
will run while jogging is active. 
Range: 0.00 to 550.00 (factory default: 5.00) 
P1059[0...2] 
JOG frequency left [Hz] 
This parameter determines the frequency at which the inverter 
will run while JOG left is selected. 
Range: 0.00 to 550.00 (factory default: 5.00)

jogging is active. 
Range: 0.00 to 650.00 (factory default: 10.00) 
5.6.2.3 
Setting the voltage boost 
Functionality 
For low output frequencies, the V/f characteristics only give a low output voltage. The ohmic 
resistances of the stator winding play a role at low frequencies, which are neglected when 
determining the motor flux in V/f control. This means that the output voltage can be too low in 
order to: 
● implement the magnetization of the asynchronous motor 
● hold the load 
● overcome losses in the system. 
The output voltage can be increased (boosted) in the inverter using the parameters as 
shown in the table below. 
 
Parameter 
Boost type 
Description 
P1310 
Continuous boost [%] 
This parameter defines boost level relative to P0305 (rated motor current) 
applicable to both linear and quadratic V/f curves. 
Range: 0.0 to 250.0 (factory default: 50.0) 
The voltage boost is effective over the complete frequency range whereby the 
value continually decreases at high frequencies.

Chapter 2
Start Up
This chapter describes how to start up the PowerFlex 4M drive. To 
simplify drive setup, the most commonly programmed parameters are 
organized in a single Basic Program Group.
Important: Read the General Precautions section before proceeding.
Before Applying Power to the Drive
❏
1. Confirm that all inputs are connected to the correct terminals and are 
secure.
❏
2. Verify that AC line power at the disconnect device is within the rated 
value of the drive.
❏
3. Verify that any digital control power is 24 volts.
❏
4. Verify that the Sink (SNK)/Source (SRC) Setup DIP switch is set to 
match your control wiring scheme. See Figure 5 for location.
Important: The default control scheme is Source (SRC). The Stop 
terminal is jumpered (I/O Terminals 01 and 11) to allow 
starting from the keypad. If the control scheme is changed 
to Sink (SNK), the jumper must be removed from I/O 
Terminals 01 and 11 and installed between I/O Terminals 
01 and 04.
!
ATTENTION: Power must be applied to the drive to perform the 
following start-up procedures. Some of the voltages present are at 
incoming line potential. To avoid electric shock hazard or damage to 
equipment, only qualified service personnel should perform the 
following procedure. Thoroughly read and understand the procedure 
before beginning. If an event does not occur while performing this 
procedure, Do Not Proceed. Remove All Power including user 
supplied control voltages. User supplied voltages may exist even when 
main AC power is not applied to the drive. Correct the malfunction 
before continuing.
Prepare For Drive Start-Up

5.6.2.4 
Setting the PID controller 
Functionality 
The integrated PID controller (technology controller) supports all kinds of simple process 
control tasks, e.g. controlling pressures, levels, or flowrates. The PID controller specifies the 
speed setpoint of the motor in such a way that the process variable to be controlled 
corresponds to its setpoint. 
 
Setting parameters 
 
Parameter 
Function 
Setting 
Main function parameters 
P2200[0...2] 
BI: Enable PID controller 
This parameter allows user to enable / disable the PID control-
ler. Setting to 1 enables the PID closed-loop controller. 
Setting 1 automatically disables normal ramp times set in 
P1120 and P1121 and the normal frequency setpoints. 
Factory default: 0 
P2235[0...2] 
BI: Enable PID-MOP (UP-cmd) 
This parameter defines source of UP command. 
Possible sources: 19.13 (BOP), 722.x (Digital Input), 2036.13 
(USS on RS485) 
P2236[0...2] 
BI: Enable PID-MOP (DOWN-cmd) 
This parameter defines source of DOWN command. 
Possible sources: 19.14 (BOP), 722.x (Digital Input), 2036.14 
(USS on RS485) 
Additional commissioning parameters 
P2251 
PID mode 
= 0: PID as setpoint (factory default) 
= 1: PID as trim source

7. Familiarize yourself with the integral keypad features (see Integral 
Keypad on page 34) before setting any Program Group parameters.
Start, Stop, Direction, and Speed Control 
Factory default parameter values allow the drive to be controlled from 
the integral keypad. No programming is required to start, stop, change 
direction and control speed directly from the integral keypad.
Important: To disable reverse operation, see A434 [Reverse Disable].
If a fault appears on power up, see Fault Descriptions on page 73 for an 
explanation of the fault code.
Variable Torque Fan/Pump Applications
For improved motor tuning performance when using a premium efficient 
motor on a variable torque load, set A453 [Boost Select] to option 2 
“35.0, VT”.

5.6.2.5 
Setting the braking function 
Functionality 
The motor can be electrically or mechanically braked by the inverter via the following brakes: 
● Electrical brakes 
– DC brake 
– Compound brake 
– Dynamic brake 
● Mechanical brake 
– Motor holding brake 
DC braking 
DC braking causes the motor to stop rapidly by applying a DC braking current (current 
applied also holds shaft stationary). For DC braking, a DC current is impressed in the stator 
winding which results in a significant braking torque for an asynchronous motor. 
DC braking is selected as follows: 
● Sequence 1: selected after OFF1 or OFF3 (the DC brake is released via P1233) 
● Sequence 2: selected directly with the BICO parameter P1230 
Sequence 1 
1. Enabled using P1233 
2. DC braking is activated with the OFF1 or OFF3 command (see figure below) 
3. The inverter frequency is ramped down along the parameterized OFF1 or OFF3 ramp 
down to the frequency at which DC braking is to start - P1234. 
4. The inverter pulses are inhibited for the duration of the de-magnetizing time P0347. 
5. The required braking current P1232 is then impressed for the selected braking time 
P1233. The status is displayed using signal r0053 bit 00. 
The inverter pulses are inhibited after the braking time has expired.

rows.title: Database-Backed Settings Service
rows.summary: Zero-downtime configuration changes via database-backed settings with environment variable fallback and 5-minute cache TTL
rows.content:
**Problem**: Changing agent behavior required code changes and service restarts, causing downtime and slow iteration.

**Solution**: Store configuration in Supabase table with environment variable fallback. Settings cached in-memory (5-minute TTL) for performance.

**Architecture**: Settings Service queries database → caches result → returns value. Fallback: Database → Environment Variable → Default Value.

**Database Schema**: agent_factory_settings table with columns: category (text), key (text), value (text), description (text), updated_at (timestamptz).

**Type Conversion**: Supports get_bool(), get_int(), get_float() with validation and warnings on parse failures.

**Runtime Changes**: Update Supabase table via UI → call settings.reload() → new values picked up without service restart.

**Pattern Origin**: Adapted from Archon's CredentialService (13.4k⭐ production system).

**Files**: agent_factory/core/settings_service.py (319 lines)

rows.title: RAG Cross-Encoder Reranking
rows.summary: Cross-encoder reranking improves search relevance from 65% to 85% (+20% absolute improvement) using ms-marco-MiniLM model
rows.content:
**Problem**: Pure keyword/vector search retrieved irrelevant documents, leading to hallucinated answers.

**Solution**: Cross-encoder reranking scores query-document pairs for better semantic relevance after initial retrieval.

**Architecture**: Initial retrieval (pgvector + full-text) → 20 candidate docs → Cross-encoder scores each (query, doc) pair → Rerank by score → Return top-k.

**Why Cross-Encoders Work Better**:
- Bi-encoder (vector search): Encodes query and documents separately, fast but less accurate
- Cross-encoder (reranking): Encodes query+document together with attention mechanism, slower but much more accurate

**Model**: cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 (90MB, ~50ms for 20 docs on CPU, ~10ms on GPU).

**Performance**: Initial retrieval 65% relevant, after reranking 85% relevant (20% absolute improvement). Total latency: 100ms (search) + 50ms (reranking) = 150ms (acceptable for async agents).

**Impact**: Better answers → higher confidence scores → fewer escalations → less human intervention.

**Files**: agent_factory/rivet_pro/rag/reranker.py (200 lines)

rows.title: LLM Router Implementation Example
rows.summary: Complete example of using LLMRouter with capability-based routing, fallback, caching, and cost tracking
rows.content:
**Use Case**: Route LLM requests to cheapest capable model with automatic failover and cost tracking.

**Implementation**: Import create_router(), configure with enable_cache and enable_fallback, use route_by_capability() for automatic model selection or complete() for explicit model.

**Features Demonstrated**:
- Capability-based routing (SIMPLE → gpt-3.5-turbo, COMPLEX → gpt-4o)
- Fallback chain configuration
- Automatic cost tracking
- Cache configuration
- Streaming support

**Cost Savings**: This pattern reduced production costs from $750/month to $198/month (73% reduction) for 50+ agents.

**Files**: agent_factory/llm/router.py, agent_factory/llm/types.py

5.6.3.13 
Running the inverter in motor staging mode 
Functionality 
Motor staging allows the control of up to 2 additional staged pumps or fans, based on a PID 
control system. The complete system consists of one pump controlled by the inverter and up 
to 2 further pumps / fans controlled from contactors or motor starters. The contactors or 
motor starter are controlled by digital outputs from the inverter. 
The diagram below shows a typical pumping system. Commissioning
